From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American jazz saxophonist (1929–91)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Rusty_Bryant "Special:EditPage/Rusty Bryant") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Rusty Bryant"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Rusty+Bryant%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Rusty+Bryant%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Rusty+Bryant%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Rusty+Bryant%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Rusty+Bryant%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Rusty+Bryant%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(November 2018)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Royal Gordon** "**Rusty**" **Bryant** (November 25, 1929 – March 25, 1991) was an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") tenor and alto saxophonist.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rusty_Bryant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Bryant was born in [Huntington, West Virginia](/wiki/Huntington,_West_Virginia "Huntington, West Virginia"), and grew up in [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"), becoming a fixture of the local jazz scene. He worked with [Tiny Grimes](/wiki/Tiny_Grimes "Tiny Grimes") and [Stomp Gordon](/wiki/Stomp_Gordon "Stomp Gordon") before founding his own ensemble, the Carolyn Club Band, in 1951. He signed with [Dot Records](/wiki/Dot_Records "Dot Records") in 1954 and released several albums as a leader in the second half of the 1950s. In 1953, his live recording "All Nite Long" (a faster version of "[Night Train](/wiki/Night_Train_(Jimmy_Forrest_composition) "Night Train (Jimmy Forrest composition)")") became a hit [R&B](/wiki/R%26B "R&B") single in the U.S.[[1]](#cite_note-amg-1)

Bryant's contract with Dot ended in 1957, and he returned to Columbus to do mostly local engagements, playing often with pianist-organist [Hank Marr](/wiki/Hank_Marr "Hank Marr"). [Nancy Wilson](/wiki/Nancy_Wilson_(jazz_singer) "Nancy Wilson (jazz singer)") also sang in his group. It was not until his appearance on the 1968 [Groove Holmes](/wiki/Groove_Holmes "Groove Holmes") album *That Healin' Feelin'* that he resurfaced beyond regional acclaim, and soon after he began leading dates for [Prestige Records](/wiki/Prestige_Records "Prestige Records"). He recorded extensively for the label from 1969 through the middle of the 1970s, being a sideman with [Ivan "Boogaloo Joe" Jones](/wiki/Ivan_%22Boogaloo_Joe%22_Jones "Ivan \"Boogaloo Joe\" Jones"), [Johnny "Hammond" Smith](/wiki/Johnny_%22Hammond%22_Smith "Johnny \"Hammond\" Smith"), [Charles Kynard](/wiki/Charles_Kynard "Charles Kynard"), and [Sonny Phillips](/wiki/Sonny_Phillips "Sonny Phillips"); his 1970 release *Soul Liberation* was his most commercially successful, reaching No. 35 on the U.S. Black Albums chart and No. 15 on the Top Jazz Albums chart. Bryant continued to record into the early 1980s, then returned to mostly local dates in Columbus. He died there on March 25, 1991.

Though they resemble and share the same surname, Rusty Bryant and jazz pianist [Ray Bryant](/wiki/Ray_Bryant "Ray Bryant") are not related.

Rusty Bryant was the father of Eric Royal Bryant, Vince Bryant, the former bassist for the Austin, Texas, group Extreme Heat,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and pop singer Stevie Woods, the latter having a moderately successful recording career in the early 1980s with the top 40 hit songs "Steal the Night" and "Just Can't Win 'Em All". Rusty was the grandfather of Tiana Woods, a Los Angeles based singer/songwriter and front woman for the band Living Eulogy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rusty_Bryant&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Discography")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section may **require [cleanup](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")** to meet Wikipedia's [quality standards](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style "Wikipedia:Manual of Style"). The specific problem is: **Non-use of wikitables, unreferenced, laundry list appearance.** Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Rusty_Bryant "Special:EditPage/Rusty Bryant") if you can. *(September 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

### Original 7" vinyl (45rpm) releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rusty_Bryant&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Original 7\" vinyl (45rpm) releases")]

* Carolyn Records [credited to Rusty Bryant And The Carolyn Club Band]:
* 45-333 (1953) "Castle Rock" [matrix 1333] // "Nite Train" [matrix 1334]
* Dot Records [all credited to Rusty Bryant And The Carolyn Club Band]:
* 45-15134 (1954) "Castle Rock" [8222] reissue // "All Nite Long" [8223] reissue of "Nite Train" with new title.
* 45-15164 (5/54) "Pink Champagne" [8254] // "Slow Drag" [8255]
* 45-15221 (8/54) "Blow Rusty Blow" [8336] // "Merry Go Round" [8325]
* 45-1229 (?/54) "House Rocker" [8333] // "Danger Blues" [8337] with Jane Turner - vocal
* 45-15324 (1/55) "Back Street" [8487] // "Record Delivery Blues" [8489]
* 45-15376 (5/55) "Hot Fudge" [8659] // "Ridin' With Rusty" [8661]
* 45-15420 (9/55) "The Honeydripper" [8658] // "Moonlight Garden Stomp" [8660]
* 45-15449 (1/56) "I Need Somebody" [9018] with Evans Sisters - vocals // "Frankie And Johnny" [9032]
* 45-15476 (6/56) "Foot Stompin'" [8199] // "Don't Tell Me" [8200]
* 45-15483 (6/56) "All Nite Long" [8223] reissue // "Pink Champagne" [8254] reissue
* 45-15494 (8/56) "Honky Tonk, Part II" [9277] // "Lonely Cryin' Heart" [9278]
* 45-15541 (1/57) "Kittyhawk, Pt. 2" [9505] // "Little Hawk's Walk" [9506]
* Prestige Records [credited to Rusty Bryant]:
* 45-728 (1969) "Zoo Boogaloo, Pt. 1" // "Zoo Boogaloo, Pt. 2"
* 45-738 (1970) "Soul Liberation, Pt. 1" // "Soul Liberation, Pt. 2"
* 45-750 (1971) "Fire Eater" // "The Hooker"

### LP/CD releases/compilations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rusty_Bryant&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: LP/CD releases/compilations")]

* *America's Greatest Jazz* ([Dot](/wiki/Dot_Records "Dot Records") DLP-3006, 1956) [credited to Rusty Bryant And The Carolyn Club Band]
* *Jazz Horizons: Rusty Bryant Plays Jazz* (Dot DLP-3079, 1958)
* *America's Greatest Jazz, Vol. II* (Dot DLP-3353, 1961)
* *[Rusty Bryant Returns](/wiki/Rusty_Bryant_Returns "Rusty Bryant Returns")* ([Prestige](/wiki/Prestige_Records "Prestige Records"), 1969)
* *[Night Train Now!](/wiki/Night_Train_Now! "Night Train Now!")* (Prestige, 1969)
* *[Soul Liberation](/wiki/Soul_Liberation "Soul Liberation")* (Prestige, 1970)
* *[Fire Eater](/wiki/Fire_Eater_(album) "Fire Eater (album)")* (Prestige, 1971) - with Wilbert Longmire
* *[Wild Fire](/wiki/Wild_Fire_(album) "Wild Fire (album)")* (Prestige, 1971) - with [Jimmy Ponder](/wiki/Jimmy_Ponder "Jimmy Ponder")
* *[Friday Night Funk for Saturday Night Brothers](/wiki/Friday_Night_Funk_for_Saturday_Night_Brothers "Friday Night Funk for Saturday Night Brothers")* (Prestige, 1972)
* *[For the Good Times](/wiki/For_the_Good_Times_(Rusty_Bryant_album) "For the Good Times (Rusty Bryant album)")* (Prestige, 1973)
* *[Until It's Time for You to Go](/wiki/Until_It%27s_Time_for_You_to_Go_(album) "Until It's Time for You to Go (album)")* (Prestige, 1974)
* *Rusty Rides Again* (Phoenix Jazz, 1980; CD reissue: [HighNote](/wiki/HighNote_Records "HighNote Records"), 2001)
* *Legends Of Acid Jazz: Rusty Bryant* (Prestige, 1996) (compilation of *Night Train Now!* + *Soul Liberation*)
* *Legends Of Acid Jazz: Rusty Bryant, Vol. 2* (Prestige, 1999) (compilation of *Fire Eater* + *Wild Fire*)
* *For The Good Times* (Prestige, 2002) (compilation of *For The Good Times* + *Until It's Time For You To Go*)
* *Original Quintet Complete Recordings* (Lone Hill Jazz, 2004) (compilation of *Jazz Horizons: Rusty Bryant Plays Jazz* + *America's Greatest Jazz, Vol. II*)
* *The Chronological Rusty Bryant 1952-1954* (Classics 'Blues & Rhythm Series' 5182, 2007)
* *America's Greatest Rock 'N' Roll* (Carolyn Records 101, 20??) [credited to Rusty Bryant And The Carolyn Club Band] -note: includes all eleven singles [A & B sides] that Bryant released on Dot Records (from 1954-1956), plus "Hanka Boo" [an album only track].

### As sideman

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rusty_Bryant&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: As sideman")]

**With [Richard "Groove" Holmes](/wiki/Richard_Holmes_(organist) "Richard Holmes (organist)")**

* *[That Healin' Feelin'](/wiki/That_Healin%27_Feelin%27_(Richard_%22Groove%22_Holmes_album) "That Healin' Feelin' (Richard \"Groove\" Holmes album)")* (Prestige, 1968)

**With [Boogaloo Joe Jones](/wiki/Ivan_%22Boogaloo_Joe%22_Jones "Ivan \"Boogaloo Joe\" Jones")**

* *[Boogaloo Joe](/wiki/Boogaloo_Joe "Boogaloo Joe")* (Prestige, 1969)
* *[Right On Brother](/wiki/Right_On_Brother "Right On Brother")* (Prestige, 1970)
* *[Snake Rhythm Rock](/wiki/Snake_Rhythm_Rock "Snake Rhythm Rock")* (Prestige, 1972)

**With [Charles Kynard](/wiki/Charles_Kynard "Charles Kynard")**

* *[Wa-Tu-Wa-Zui (Beautiful People)](/wiki/Wa-Tu-Wa-Zui_(Beautiful_People) "Wa-Tu-Wa-Zui (Beautiful People)")* (Prestige, 1970)

**With [Hank Marr](/wiki/Hank_Marr "Hank Marr")**

* *Teentime: Latest Dance Steps* (King, 1963)
* *Live at the Club 502* (King, 1964)
* *On and Off Stage* (King, 1965)
* *[Sounds from the Marr-Ket Place](/wiki/Sounds_from_the_Marr-Ket_Place "Sounds from the Marr-Ket Place")* (King, 1968)

**With [Jimmy McGriff](/wiki/Jimmy_McGriff "Jimmy McGriff")**

* *[The Starting Five](/wiki/The_Starting_Five "The Starting Five")* (Milestone, 1987)

**With [Sonny Phillips](/wiki/Sonny_Phillips "Sonny Phillips")**

* *[Black on Black!](/wiki/Black_on_Black! "Black on Black!")* (Prestige, 1970)

**With [Johnny "Hammond" Smith](/wiki/Johnny_%22Hammond%22_Smith "Johnny \"Hammond\" Smith")**

* *[Soul Talk](/wiki/Soul_Talk_(Johnny_%22Hammond%22_Smith_album) "Soul Talk (Johnny \"Hammond\" Smith album)")* (Prestige, 1969)
* *[Black Feeling!](/wiki/Black_Feeling! "Black Feeling!")* (Prestige, 1969)
